unit.name: RLHF reward modeling and KL control
unit.description: Introduces preference optimization, Bradley-Terry reward modeling, reward hacking, and KL regularization.
unit.summary: RLHF is introduced as an attempt to satisfy human preferences directly rather than rely on token prediction [ts=2347s] [ts=2394s]. Using summarization, the instructor writes the desired objective as maximizing expected reward over outputs sampled from the language model; this is different from pretraining and SFT because the model samples its own completions and the reward may be non-differentiable [ts=2449s] [ts=2498s]. The pipeline is instruction tuning, reward-model estimation, and then optimization of the language model against that reward model [ts=2503s] [ts=2548s]. Since absolute human scores are noisy and uncalibrated, humans instead compare outputs and rank which completion is better [ts=2694s] [ts=2777s]. Bradley-Terry modeling turns pairwise preferences into a loss where preference probability is the sigmoid of the reward difference between winning and losing completions [ts=2822s] [ts=2889s]. A learned reward can be hacked, so the model is penalized for drifting too far from the initial model using a beta log-ratio term whose expectation is KL divergence [ts=3034s] [ts=3183s]. RLHF then samples completions, scores them, and updates probabilities toward high-reward regions [ts=3268s] [ts=3301s].

key_points.text: RLHF optimizes expected human preference reward over model-sampled outputs.
The RLHF pipeline is instruction tuning, reward model estimation, and reward optimization.
Pairwise ranking is easier and more stable than absolute human scoring.
Bradley-Terry models preference probability as sigmoid of the reward difference.
Optimizing a learned reward model can cause reward hacking.
A beta log-ratio penalty keeps the optimized model close to the initial model; its expectation is KL divergence.

Using summarization, the instructor writes the desired objective as maximizing expected reward over outputs sampled from the language model; this is different from pretraining and SFT because the model samples its own completions and the reward may be non-differentiable [ts=2449s] [ts=2498s]. The pipeline is instruction tuning, reward-model estimation, and then optimization of the language model against that reward model [ts=2503s] [ts=2548s]. Since absolute human scores are noisy and uncalibrated, humans instead compare outputs and rank which completion is better [ts=2694s] [ts=2777s]. Bradley-Terry modeling turns pairwise preferences into a loss where preference probability is the sigmoid of the reward difference between winning and losing completions [ts=2822s] [ts=2889s]. A learned reward can be hacked, so the model is penalized for drifting too far from the initial model using a beta log-ratio term whose expectation is KL divergence [ts=3034s] [ts=3183s]. RLHF then samples completions, scores them, and updates probabilities toward high-reward regions [ts=3268s] [ts=3301s].

kp_catalog.name: RLHF preference optimization
kp_catalog.description: The RLHF reward-maximization objective with KL regularization and Bradley-Terry-style pairwise rewards. Optimizing language models for learned human preference rewards over model-generated outputs.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because rLHF preference optimization formalizes how the RLHF reward-maximization objective with KL regularization and Bradley-Terry-style pairwise rewards, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Reward modeling from pairwise preferences
kp_catalog.description: Turning pairwise human preferences into a reward-model objective based on reward differences. Pairwise reward-model training and RewardBench as local evaluation for reward models.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because reward modeling from pairwise preferences formalizes how turning pairwise human preferences into a reward-model objective based on reward differences, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Reward hacking and KL regularization
kp_catalog.description: The risk of exploiting learned rewards and the KL-style penalty that keeps the policy near the reference model. Optimizers exploiting incomplete reward specifications in reward-model-based alignment.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because reward hacking and KL regularization formalizes how the risk of exploiting learned rewards and the KL-style penalty that keeps the policy near the reference model, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context while roles split between gateway and supporting and importance labels split between critical and high.
